title: Basic Linux Commands
description: Introduction to Linux and fundamental terminal commands

tasks.title: Open Jupyter and Create Python Notebook
tasks.sonic_prompt: Watch as I open Jupyter Notebook and create a new Python notebook.

subtasks.title: Open Jupyter in browser
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Opening Jupyter Notebook in the browser.
subtasks.goal: Navigate to http://localhost:8888 to open Jupyter Notebook. The Jupyter file browser should appear.

subtasks.title: Create a new Python notebook
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Now I will create a new Python notebook.
subtasks.goal: On the Jupyter file browser page, click the 'New' button on the top-right side, then click 'Python 3 (ipykernel)' from the dropdown to create a new notebook.

subtasks.title: Write and run Python code
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Typing a simple Python program and running it.
subtasks.goal: In the new Jupyter notebook, click on the first empty code cell, type: print(2+3) then press Shift+Enter to run it. The output 5 should appear below the cell.

tasks.title: Understanding the Linux Terminal
tasks.slide_context: The Linux terminal, also called the command-line interface or CLI, is a text window where you type commands directly to the operating system. It is faster and more powerful than a graphical interface for most tasks. Every Linux command follows the pattern: command name, options, then arguments. For example, ls -la /home lists all files including hidden ones in the home directory.

tasks.title: Hands-On: Linux Commands
tasks.sonic_prompt: Now let us put everything into practice. I will demonstrate each command live on this machine. Watch the terminal carefully.

subtasks.title: Open the Linux Terminal
subtasks.sonic_prompt: First, let me open the Linux terminal. This is where all our commands will run.

subtasks.title: Run the ls command
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Now watch as I run the ls command. It lists every file and folder in the current directory. You will use this constantly.

subtasks.title: Run the pwd command
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Next is pwd — print working directory. It shows exactly where you are in the file system right now.

tasks.title: Start Jupyter Notebook
tasks.sonic_prompt: Now I will start Jupyter Notebook on this machine. Watch the terminal as I launch it.

subtasks.title: Start Jupyter Notebook server
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Starting the Jupyter Notebook server. This will take a moment.

tasks.title: Create and Run Python Code in Jupyter
tasks.sonic_prompt: Now watch as I open Jupyter Notebook in the browser and write Python code.

subtasks.title: Open Jupyter in browser
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Opening Jupyter Notebook in the browser.
subtasks.goal: Navigate to http://localhost:8888 to open Jupyter Notebook. The Jupyter file browser should appear.

subtasks.title: Create a new Python notebook
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Now I will create a new Python notebook by clicking the New button.
subtasks.goal: On the Jupyter file browser page, click the 'New' button on the right side, then click 'Python 3 (ipykernel)' from the dropdown to create a new notebook.

subtasks.title: Write and run Python code
subtasks.sonic_prompt: Finally, I will type a simple Python program and run it.
subtasks.goal: In the new Jupyter notebook, click on the first empty code cell, type: print(2+3) then press Shift+Enter to run it. The output 5 should appear below the cell.
